title: Embossed patterns in wet sheets
subtitle: Pressing textures and shapes into freshly formed paper before drying
excerpt: Wet handmade paper can accept impressions from almost any textured object: mesh, lace, leaves, rubber stamps, or carved foam. As the paper dries around the embedded texture, it takes on the impression permanently. This tutorial covers pattern embossing at the wet-sheet stage, including how to handle the sheet to keep it intact.
sourceNotes: Helen Hiebert, Papermaking with Plants (1998, Storey), chapter on embedded and embossed papers. Technique of pressing textures into wet sheets is standard in artist-papermaking practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pressing a textured object into freshly-formed, still-wet paper so the impression becomes permanent as the sheet dries around it.
A surface that a wet paper sheet will not stick to during embossing, typically a piece of waxed paper, plastic sheeting, or a non-stick mat laid between the sheet and the embossing object.

content.text: A freshly-couched sheet is still plastic: the fibres have not yet bonded. At this stage, before any pressing or significant drying, the sheet can accept impressions from objects placed on its surface. The impression is locked in as the fibres dry and mat together around the shape.

Best results come from objects with sharp edges or pronounced texture: metal mesh, dried plant matter (skeletal leaves, ferns), lace, carved foam, or carved plaster. Soft rubber craft stamps work if pressed firmly. Smooth objects with no relief do not produce a visible impression. Avoid objects that absorb water (untreated wood), they swell and stick to the wet sheet.
After couching the wet sheet onto its felt or board, while the sheet is still very wet, lay your embossing object face-down onto the surface. Cover with

(a sheet of waxed paper works well), then apply firm hand pressure for 30 seconds. For a deeper impression, set a press board and heavy weight on top and leave for 5-10 minutes. Remove the release material and then the embossing object by peeling straight up. The
will be visible immediately, and will sharpen as the sheet dries.

To emboss a repeated pattern across the sheet, make a simple registration frame: cut a window in a piece of card the same size as the mould, and mark grid positions for the embossing object. Place the frame over the sheet to position each impression before pressing. The slightly longer drying time while you register each impression is fine, the sheet remains plastic for up to 20 minutes after couching.

Embossed sheets work well as letterhead paper (the embossed area is left blank for writing or printing), as decorative wrapping paper, and as book-cover paper. The embossed texture affects how ink sits on the surface: a deep impression creates a valley that collects ink differently from the raised ridges around it, giving a two-tone effect even when writing in a single colour.
